title: If it has a .speak() method, it works
instruction:
Python's philosophy: 'if it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck.'

You do not need to declare that your class implements an interface. If it has the right methods, it works with any function that expects those methods.

Run this. `make_speak()` works with both Dog and Cat — no shared parent, no interface declaration. They just both have a `speak()` method.
successMessage: No shared parent, no interface declaration — it just works. This is duck typing: behaviour defined by what an object can do, not what class it comes from.

title: ABC — enforce the contract
instruction:
Duck typing is flexible but informal. What if you want to **enforce** that every subclass must implement certain methods?

That's what ABCs (Abstract Base Classes) are for. If a subclass doesn't implement every `@abstractmethod`, creating an instance raises a `TypeError`.

Add a `Circle` class that implements `area()` and `perimeter()`.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Both Rectangle and Circle implement the Shape contract. Forget to implement area() or perimeter() and Python will tell you the moment you try to instantiate the class.

title: ABCs prevent instantiation
instruction:
Abstract classes are blueprints only — you cannot create an instance of one.

Run this to see the error. It's a clear signal: implement the abstract methods first.
successMessage: Can't instantiate abstract class Vehicle with abstract method fuel_type. The blueprint can't become a thing on its own — you must subclass it and implement the required methods.

title: Protocol — the modern approach
instruction:
`Protocol` (from `typing`) is Python 3.8+'s answer to interfaces. Unlike ABCs, you do NOT inherit from it.

Any class that has the right methods satisfies the Protocol — no relationship needed.

Run this.
successMessage: Neither Circle nor Square inherits from Drawable. They just happen to have a draw() method. Protocol checks the shape of the object — structural typing.

title: @runtime_checkable — use isinstance with Protocols
instruction:
By default, Protocols are only checked by type-checkers (like mypy), not at runtime.

Add `@runtime_checkable` and `isinstance()` starts working:

```python
@runtime_checkable
class Saveable(Protocol):
    def save(self) -> None: ...
```

Add `@runtime_checkable` to make the isinstance checks work.
starter:
from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable

# TODO: add @runtime_checkable to this Protocol
class Serializable(Protocol):
    def to_json(self) -> str: ...

class User:
    def to_json(self) -> str:
        return '{"type": "user"}'

class Product:  # no to_json
    pass

u = User()
p = Product()
print(isinstance(u, Serializable))  # should be True
print(isinstance(p, Serializable))  # should be False

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: isinstance works with the Protocol now. User has to_json so it passes. Product doesn't so it fails. No inheritance needed — just the right methods.

title: ABC vs Protocol — know when to use which
instruction:
Use **ABC** when:
- You control all the classes
- You want to share code in the base class
- You want Python to enforce implementation

Use **Protocol** when:
- You don't control all the classes (third-party code, built-ins)
- You want structural/duck-typing behaviour with type safety

Fix the code: `Storable` checks for dict-like behaviour, but `dict` can't inherit from our ABC. Use Protocol instead.

codeChecks.message: Add @runtime_checkable so isinstance works at runtime.

successMessage: dict satisfies Storable because it has keys() and __getitem__ — structural subtyping. ABCs need inheritance. Protocols just need the right methods.

title: Build a plugin system
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a plugin system using Protocol:
- `Plugin(Protocol)` with methods `load()` and `process(data: str) -> str`
- `UpperPlugin` — converts text to uppercase
- `ReversePlugin` — reverses the text
- `run_pipeline(plugins, data)` — applies all plugins in sequence

Test: `run_pipeline([UpperPlugin(), ReversePlugin()], "hello")` → `"OLLEH"`

codeChecks.message: Define process() in the Protocol and both plugins.

successMessage: A Protocol-based plugin pipeline! Add any new plugin — it works without touching run_pipeline. This is how every extensible framework is built. Chapter 18 done!
